title: Cat Temporary Blindness After Waking Up: Causes & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat disoriented after waking? Learn about temporary blindness, when to seek veterinary care, and how to help your pet during an episode.
content_html:
TL;DR: Temporary blindness in cats after waking can indicate serious underlying issues like high blood pressure or neurological problems; schedule a vet visit within 24–48 hours or seek emergency care if symptoms persist.

What causes temporary blindness in cats when they first wake up?

Temporary blindness after waking up, or transient vision loss, occurs when a cat appears disoriented, bumps into objects, or has fixed, dilated pupils immediately after a nap. This phenomenon can be a symptom of underlying health issues such as systemic hypertension (high blood pressure), neurological disturbances, or metabolic changes that affect the retina or the brain's ability to process visual information.

Is my cat having a veterinary emergency if they experience temporary vision loss?

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours for a full diagnostic workup if your cat’s vision returns to normal within a few minutes and they are behaving normally otherwise.
  • Seek emergency veterinary care immediately if the blindness is accompanied by signs of distress, seizures, or if the vision does not return to prevent permanent damage.

How can providing photos and videos of the episode help with my cat's triage?

  • Capture a video of your cat's behavior during the episode to help the vet observe symptoms that may resolve before you reach the clinic.
  • Take a close-up photo of their eyes to document pupil size, eye symmetry, and any involuntary movements (nystagmus).
  • Provide these visuals to help the vet differentiate between a primary eye problem and a neurological or cardiovascular issue.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, acute blindness after waking up can be attributed to retinal detachment secondary to systemic hypertension or intraocular hemorrhage. Intracranial diseases such as pituitary masses, paranasal masses, meningiomas, and lymphosarcoma affecting the optic chiasm can also cause acute blindness. Central blindness arising from issues in the optic tracts or occipital cortex due to cardiac arrest during anesthesia, seizures, or severe head trauma should also be considered. Evaluation should include assessment of pupillary light reflexes (PLR) and investigation into potential systemic causes.

Chapter: Neurology, Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: Can high blood pressure cause temporary blindness in cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, systemic hypertension is a common cause of vision changes in cats, especially seniors. It can cause the retina to partially detach or lead to small bleeds in the eye.

faqs.question: What should I do while my cat is experiencing vision loss?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat calm and confined to a safe, carpeted area. Avoid moving furniture or picking them up abruptly, as this can increase their disorientation and stress.

faqs.question: Will my cat's vision return to normal?
faqs.answer: Whether the vision loss is permanent depends on the underlying cause. If it is caught early and the primary condition (like high blood pressure) is managed, many cats can regain or maintain their sight.
